prompt: The programmer expects this to print 'Animal speaks Dog barks' but it prints 'Dog barks Dog barks'. What is the bug?
choices: Cast a to Animal before the first call: ((Animal) a).speak()
Dynamic dispatch always uses the runtime type — a refers to a Dog, so both calls invoke Dog.speak()
Remove @Override so the compiler treats Dog.speak() as a separate method
The Animal and Dog classes must be in separate files for the cast to take effect
explanation: In Java, overridden method calls are resolved at runtime using the actual object's type, not the declared variable type. 'a' refers to a Dog instance, so a.speak() always calls Dog.speak() — the declared type Animal has no effect at runtime. To call Animal.speak() from outside, you need an actual Animal instance. From inside Dog, you can call super.speak().
